text: The surgeon lifts the stomach cranially and dissects the gastrocolic ligament up to the short gastric vessels, which are preserved. The right gastroepiploic artery is dissected one centimeter from its origin and divided, leaving the majority of retropancreatic lymph nodes attached to the specimen.

The surgeon tunnels the portal vein by following the middle colic and gastroepiploic vessels, exploring the confluence of the superior mesenteric vein with the portal vein at the neck of the pancreas. Harmonic shears and a suction cannula are used for blunt dissection, with careful progression through the pancreatic parenchyma in case of inflammatory adhesions.

The surgeon transects the stomach proximal to the antrum using endo-GIA staplers. The gastroduodenal artery (GDA) is dissected out, and vascular clips are applied before it is divided. Small tributaries of the portal vein are identified, sutured, and divided prior to addressing the GDA.

The surgeon performs a wide takedown of the hepatic colonic flexure, extending medially to the origin of the right colonic vessels and sometimes down to the cecum. This is followed by an extensive Cattell-Braasch maneuver, detaching the head of the pancreas from the retroperitoneal space. The left side of the aorta serves as the lateral edge landmark, with the left renal vein and the origin of the superior mesenteric artery marking the completion of the maneuver. Enlarged lymph nodes in the inter-aortocaval space are harvested during the lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon performs a division of the duodenal-jejunal flexure and right-sided D-rotation of the duodenum, extensively mobilizing the fourth part of the duodenum. The first jejunal loop is divided using endo-GIA staplers, followed by an enterocrate cholecystectomy down to the cystic duct.

The surgeon divides the neck of the pancreas anteriorly to the portal vein using harmonic shears, ensuring optimal exposure of the inferior and superior margins. The pancreatic duct is identified and cannulated with an endoluminal stent. If the duct is inadvertently sealed by thermal spread, it is reopened and stented.

The surgeon performs the uncinate process dissection using a right-to-left approach, dissecting from below upwards. Hemostatic clips are applied to control bleeding from Henle's trunk, followed by division with a harmonic scalpel. The superior mesenteric vein is retracted to the left using a suction cannula to enhance visualization of the surgical field posterior to the vein.

The surgeon carefully dissects the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery circumferentially using a harmonic scalpel and secures it with hemostatic clips to prevent retraction and control bleeding. The surgical field may present complex situations such as bulky tumors of the pancreatic head or anatomical variations affecting the confluence of the superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein.

The surgeon encounters vascular abnormalities, including a replaced right hepatic artery type III, accessory right hepatic artery, and replaced celiac trunk during the dissection along the uncinate process. The dissection is divided into three layers: the anterior portal layer with tributaries clipped and cut, the arterial layer with inferior and superior pancreatic or duodenal arteries, and the posterior lymphatic layer managed with a harmonic scalpel. Dissection along the superior mesenteric vein is performed using hemostatic clips and harmonic scalpel to prevent bleeding. The common bile duct is divided cranially to the cystic duct origin using cold scissors or diathermy, depending on duct thickness.

The surgeon completes the dissection and places the specimen in an endobag. Reconstruction begins with a jejunal loop passed through the mesocolic route to the upper quadrants for digestive reconstruction. The hepaticojejunostomy is performed in an end-to-side fashion using running sutures for the posterior row and interrupted stitches for the anterior row.

The surgeon reconstructs the anterior layer of the hepaticojejunostomy, starting from the right side of the bile duct. Stitches are placed one millimeter apart, and a running suture is used for the anterior row if the bile duct is very dilated and thick.

The surgeon performs a pancreaticojejunostomy using a robotic approach, which provides a magnified view similar to a surgical microscope. Depending on the pancreatic parenchyma texture and duct diameter, either a gland-to-mucosa or duct-to-mucosa anastomosis is chosen. For a firm pancreas and duct larger than four millimeters, the duct-to-mucosa technique is used, anchoring the posterior capsule of the pancreatic stump to the jejunal serosa with polypropylene sutures. A small opening in the jejunal mucosa is created, and the pancreatic duct is anastomosed with interrupted PDS stitches, with a stent placed inside the duct. The anterior aspect of the pancreatic capsule is fixed to the jejunal with additional sutures.

The surgeon performs a gastrojejunostomy on the same jejunal loop as the biliary anastomosis, positioned forty centimeters distally. An omega loop gastrojejunostomy is created using inter-GIA staplers, and the enterotomy site is closed with continuous Vicryl or PDS 3-0 sutures after a nasojejunal tube is passed into the jejunal limb.
The specimen is extracted through a small upper midline incision. Three surgical drains are placed: one near the pancreatic anastomosis, one close to the biliary anastomosis, and one in the pelvis.
